plan: Trim SPY to 20% to meet concentration limits and reduce overall exposure. Sell SOXL and SPAL entirely to eliminate leveraged ETF risk. Increase QQQI to 25% for more income and stability. Buy XLK at 5% for quality tech exposure. Hold 15% cash for capital preservation and buffer.
conservative.read: This plan targets a ~1.5% monthly return with a focus on capital preservation. Risk is significantly reduced by eliminating leveraged ETFs and building a cash buffer, leading to lower potential drawdowns.

plan: Trim SPY to 25% to align with concentration limits. Reduce SOXL to 3% and sell SPAL to lower overall leverage. Increase QQQI to 20% for a balanced income component. Add to CRWD, a strong performer, to 15%. Buy ZS at 5%, a high-scoring scanner setup. Maintain a 10% cash buffer.
balanced.read: This balanced plan aims for ~2.5% monthly growth by managing risk and leveraging quality. It reduces overall leverage while selectively adding to strong performers and high-quality setups. Expected drawdowns are moderate.

plan: Trim SPY to 25% to meet concentration limits. Increase SOXL to 8% and add TQQQ at 7% to lean into leveraged tech exposure. Sell SPAL, an underperforming leveraged ETF. Increase CRWD to 20% and buy ZS at 10% to concentrate in high-performing tech. Maintain a 5% cash buffer.
aggressive.read: This aggressive plan targets ~4% monthly growth by leaning into leverage and high-scoring setups. This approach amplifies both potential upside and downside, with significant risk of larger drawdowns and daily decay in leveraged ETFs. Strict stop discipline is crucial.

plan: Trim SPY to 25% and QQQ to 10% to reduce growth exposure. Maximize QQQI to 30% for increased monthly distributions. Sell SMH and CRWD to exit high-beta sectors and single names. Sell SOXL and SPAL to eliminate all leveraged ETF exposure. Hold a 20% cash buffer for stability.
income.read: This income-focused plan aims for ~1% monthly distributions by prioritizing yield and low drawdown. It significantly reduces growth and high-beta exposure, offering a more stable, cash-like experience. Distributions are variable, and NAV can still decline in market downturns.
